sentences.text: Grinims.
My name is Dorothy Grinims.
I was born in Ostrog, Poland, February 16th, 1925.
My father's name was Lazar Zeigerson, Z-E-I-G-E-R-S-O-N.
My mother's name was Syma, S-Y-M-A.
My-- basically my father was a Talmudic scholar, and very Orthodox.
And he was greatly occupied with the synagogues.
He was not an ordained rabbi, but he studied to be one.
And he was a-- had a small factory, manufacturing chairs and tables.
He employed maybe two or three people.
In those days you didn't have big businesses.
And [INAUDIBLE].
Yes.
Yeah.
Orthodox.
Yes.
Yes.
Yes.
I was a-- I went to Hebrew school and I studied religion.
And I was very observant.
I had three brothers and one sister.
Two brothers immigrated to the United States in the '20s.
And I was raised with my-- my brother Sidney and my sister Leah.
It was not a large house, but it was a very comfortable house.
And we had a very nice home life.
There was a lot of culture, a lot of religion, and basically very decent, honest, moral people.
Yes.
My brother-- we didn't have toys like my granddaughter has today.
But we had like a yo-yo, a ball.
My-- my father always used to-- I loved to play ball outside.
So I was very athletic.
And so I played ball.
And then bicycle riding and sleigh riding and ice skating.
And that's all I can remember about my childhood.
It was a-- was a town, not big.
It was a-- the population was 30,000, 10,000 Jews, and the rest Ukrainians and Polish.
It was on the Russian border, a few miles on the Russian border, yes.
The Jewish community was very close.
We had a large Jewish community.
And we had many synagogues and we had yeshivas.
We had a-- Zionist organizations, many.
And I was [INAUDIBLE] within a Zionist organization as a-- as a young girl, yes.
I went to Hebrew school.
And first I went to a-- to a Polish school.
All the subjects were in Polish.
And later, high school was Hebrew.
All the subjects were in Hebrew.
Very much so, yes, identity as a Jew, yes.
Yes.
We observed all the Jewish holidays.
It was-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it was like, in what respect?
No.
Just-- just the five of us, the five, two brothers who were here when I was growing up.

And we used to gather at a table and celebrate [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Passover, and Rosh Hashanah, Yom Kippur, and all the other-- Sukkos, my father used to build a sukkot outside the house and we ate in the sukkot.
So it was basically a very Jewish traditional family.
Yes.
I had many non-Jewish friends from this Polish school.
And I had a lot of Jewish friends.
I always liked to be surrounded with many people.
My life started to become very tragic from the age of 14.
That's when-- when the war broke out.
In 1939, September 1st, 1939, when the Russians occupied our town and part of the eastern part of Poland, and that's when my life changed.
What happened?
We were sound asleep at 4 o'clock in the morning.
We heard tanks in-- in the main road.
And everybody was extremely frightened.
It was unexpectedly.
And we woke up and got dressed and we went out to see what was going on.
And we saw-- all of a sudden, we saw Russian soldiers on tanks in the-- in the street.
They didn't bother anybody.
They were very friendly.
They gave out cigarettes and they gave out matches.
And we didn't know what happened.
But then we found out that's when the war started, well, that's when Hitler made a pact with Stalin that Russia would take over eastern part and Germany the western part of Poland.
They came in the tanks and they occupied our city.
And at that time, it became-- we were under the Russian domination.
Domination?
They changed that they immediately were arresting and sending to Siberia, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] well-to-do people, businesspeople.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] They were persecuting the Zionists.
Many of them fled.
They were persecuting the well-to-do people.
Only the working people had very good.
No.
School started again, school immediately.
Everything from Polish school or Hebrew school, everything was a-- a transition to Russian, Russian schools.
Everything was in Russian.
We had to learn Russian right away.
What was like?
It was awful, because we didn't have any money.
We didn't have an income.
There were lines all night.
We had to stay in lines for food.
I remember I was 14 years old and-- and I was on line all night in cold weather to get sugar and-- and essentials, just sugar and-- and milk and bread.
It was very, very bad.
It was-- we were-- that's the time when we-- our starvation practically started.
We had very little food.
And of course everyone was very fearful, didn't know what they're going to do to us.
But we as children had-- we had very-- a very nice life, I would say.
There was a lot of dancing and singing and then going to school.
And they really tried to indoctrinate Communism into us.
Yes.
From 1939 'til '41, two years, we are under Russian occupation.
Oh, I go to temple yes.
You did?
Now?
Oh, in-- no.
Everything stopped.
No.
Secretly, yes.
Yes.
For that reason, the Christians didn't practice either, because, you know, atheism.
They were there for two years.
And then the war broke out between Russia and Germany.
And then one day we woke up and then we saw parachutes and Germans landing from the parachutes.
And everybody started to escape.
The Russians tried to save the people, mainly Jewish people.
And they chased them out of their homes to go to Russia.
But we remained in our house.
My brother and sister escaped to Russia on a-- on a truck.
And my-- and I decided I'll stay with my parents.
I felt somebody should be with them.
Nobody believed at the time what the Nazis are going to do to the Jews.
We just thought it's just a temporary war.
And so we-- we remained, my father and I, and my mother.
And I have to stop, because I'm getting very nervous.
Hmm?
Yeah.
Excuse me.
[DRINKS]
You're telling about living with your parents.
Yes.
That was when the-- yes.
My brother and sister escaped, and I lived with my parents.
And then-- then there was a six-day-- six-day fighting in our town.
They escaped to Russia.
Right.
Then-- then it-- then the war was on.
There were fights.
It was a-- it was a war between Russia and Germany.
Yes.
Yes.
Yes.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Our town was completely engulfed in flames.
We were running from corner to corner.
Every place they turned around, all fires, because they bombarded fiercely our town.
The reason for it was because our town was a strategic place for them, because it was at the border with Russia.
That's why the Germans fought.
And the Russians, they didn't-- Russians didn't want to give up the town, Ostrog.
And I witnessed from my window street fights.
I saw Russian soldiers-- soldiers and German soldiers.
And they were fighting with bayonets, And then when the-- when the Russians-- when the Germans left, the Russian soldiers were dying in front of our-- my house.
And they were wounded and they were crying in Russian, [RUSSIAN].
That means water.
And-- and I tried to get out and give them water.
At this point, I heard shots again, and I went in.
And then he died practically-- from my window I saw him die in front of my eyes.
This-- this moment I'll never forget.
And-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
Yeah.
I'll try to remember now.
I was about 16 years, two years, right, 16.
And then we-- and then the Russians defeated and then the Germans took over our town.
And we were already under the Russian-- under the Germans.
The Germans, and then it started, the-- you want me to tell you what the Germans did at that point?
There was-- they formed the Jewish committee.
And through this Jewish committee, they gave out orders, the-- to the Jewish people to deliver all the gold and silver, leather to the German city government, which we did.
And after that, all the Jews were forced to wear yellow patches.
So we all had to wear a yellow patch in front and on the back for identity, the Jewish identity.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] And after a month later started the first execution, first [NON-ENGLISH].
Yes.
Right.
You-- you-- slowly they prepared us for everything.
You were just in a shock, [INAUDIBLE].
You just don't think.
We didn't think.
I don't remember thinking.
You would just fear and you had to obey orders, or else you were told if you're not going to wear it, you'll be shot.
I don't remember.
First-- first with the yellow patches, then rations.
We didn't have-- we didn't have food.
We were only allowed like a loaf of bread to a family.
And they were just-- terrorized us.
And of course, the Ukrainians collaborated at the time with the Germans.
And they formed the Ukrainian police, not all Ukrainians, of course, but many did.
And the Ukrainian police, they-- they were coming to Jewish homes and just terrorized, you know.
They didn't-- there was no mass-- mass killings.
Yes.
They came to our house and they searched in the closets and they opened up doors and they-- just walking around.
That was it.
Just looking for-- for leather, for money, for gold, for silver, so forth.
And then a month later started the first [NON-ENGLISH], the first execution.
And we woke up one morning and we heard footsteps.
Those were notorious footsteps of the Nazis.
And they were screaming in the streets, [GERMAN].
That means get out.
And they chased everybody.
This was like a death march.
They chased us all to this-- to the forests where the first execution was [INAUDIBLE].
Can I take one break for a second?
No?
I'm no good.
I didn't get enough sleep.
[DRINKS]
They chased everybody, chased my mother and father.
And I ran down.
I ran away.
And one Nazi caught me.
My father and mother had gone.
And because I tried to run away, he placed a machine gun in the back of me.
And for about a mile, a mile and a half, he was in the back of me, escorting me to the forest.
I was alone.
And I saw from distance there were-- and as I was walking, marching, I was stepping-- I stepped on all the furs, jewelry, because people took their possessions.
They thought maybe they're being relocated.
But they made them drop everything, so when they got to this place, they were-- they didn't have any possessions with them.
And I marched with this Nazi.
And then I came to the place.
I found my mother.
My father was already separated.
They immediately took the older men, the religious people, the sick people from hospitals, and they separated them from the women and children.
So I was all day with my mother on a very hot day, in the hot sun, no water, no food, just standing and screaming and crying.
And everybody else did.
And I remember distinctly black birds flying all over us.
And I asked my mother, what are all the black birds doing here?
And she said, that's a bad sign.
That means they're going to kill us here.
Black birds usually come when there is a tragedy, when there is disaster.
And we were there from early in the morning until late in the evening.
And then 'til-- we were surrounded by all the Nazis, all-- and Ukrainian police.
And then an order came that the women and children can go home.
And my mother and I went back to the house.
And my father was no longer with us.
And then the next morning, I got up-- got up very early.
And I-- I heard that they took some people to a-- to a different area town, like the prison.
As I-- as I was walking and crying, I was approached by a Ukrainian policeman.
His name was Lusik Stepaniuk.
He had a rifle in the back-- on the back.
He was on a bicycle.
And he stopped me.
He asked me, why are you crying?
Where are you going?
I said, my father, I'm looking for my father.
We were told that-- last night that some people were taken to-- to prison.
He says, don't go there.
They were all shot.
I was there.
They were all killed.
And then he asked me where I lived.
And then he said he'll come over in the evening.
He came in the evening.
And he befriended me and my mother and my neighbors.
And he said, I'll be coming every night to tell you, to give you a warning if there was going to be another execution, which they expect to-- to happen.
And then he brought over his mother.
And he said to his mother that-- that if anything should happen to him, that she should save me and my mother.
And she brought us food.
And-- [INAUDIBLE].
And then we lived in this house, my mother and I, with my neighbors, friends, until the second execution.
That was a few-- a few months later.
Yeah.
Yeah.
Yeah.
You are numb.
You're shocked.
It's-- you're just-- you're just-- it's fear.
You're just-- your heart palpates and you just don't know anything.
You're like in a-- like unconscious.
I-- it's very hard to explain.
It's such horrendous fear.
It's such a horrible feeling that I-- I never-- I never had this feeling again in my life.
It's the worst feeling you can imagine.
Back in the house.
And then there was a second execution.
And then they chased everybody out again of the houses.
And then my mother was taken first.
And I was able-- I was separated from my mother and I ran.
And I opened the door from the basement.
And I--
Do you want to say something?
They chased-- they chased them-- I didn't know where they chased-- where-- where to, because I remained in the house.
They got my mother first.
And I was able to run into the cellar.
And I closed the door on the inside of the cellar, so they were able-- they knew that somebody's there, because I should've left the door open, which I didn't.
I left it-- I locked from the inside in the cellar.
And I was sitting in the cellar.
And-- and then I heard footsteps in the house.
Again, [GERMAN].
And then somebody tried to open the door from the cellar.
And he was banging with the rifle.
At this point, I felt it's no use.
My mother was gone and everybody else is gone.
I might as well go to.
I I felt there's no point to-- to remain alive.
As I came out from the cellar, in front of me was this Ukrainian policeman with a big rifle.
And he said, out.
And I went on my knees and I cried.
And I said please, let me live.
I want to live.
I'm only 16 years old.
And he said, go back into the cellar but leave the door open.
He remained in the house.
Then several Nazis-- I heard the footsteps-- several Nazis came in into the house.
And they asked him in German, is everybody [GERMAN]?
And he said yes, everybody's [GERMAN].
And they left.
And he left.
I was all day in the cellar.
And then towards the evening, I said, I'm going to get myself out and I don't want to live.
As I ran out through the back of the cellar, I saw my mother running up the hill.
And she was black.
Her face was black.
I never saw her look like this.
And she said, they took all your friends, all the young people they took on trucks.
And she was screaming and crying.
And we embraced.
And we came back to the house.
This was the second execution, where they took all the young people, all my friends, all my young relatives they took on trucks and we didn't know where they took them.
Some say that they were gassed in the trucks and some say they were shot.
This, I don't have no recollection of that.
That was the second [NON-ENGLISH].
And after that, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we lived in fear.
Excuse me?
Yes.
We went out of the house, yes, because after that, an order came out they're going to place us in a ghetto.
We had to pack our belongings and move to a ghetto, which was a-- a neighborhood where very poor Jews lived years ago.
And there was this big, very famous synagogue, [NON-ENGLISH], it was called, [NON-ENGLISH].
Maharsha.
And we lived in the ghetto.
It was just called Ostrog ghetto.
Living in the ghetto?
Yeah.
We were-- we-- they gave us one room and a kitchen to share with-- with other people.
It was a small room and my mother and I slept in one room.
And we were practically starving.
There was no food.
Whoever tried to escape from the ghetto to get food, they were shot in front of that synagogue.
All the executions were in front of the synagogue, because we were told that this one was shot and all our other friends were shot in front of the synagogue, this big [NON-ENGLISH].
And there was no food.
And it was despair.
And it was hunger, was-- was horrendous.
It was-- it's impossible to describe.
And we lived-- it was a day to day, an hour from hour, a day to day existence, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and-- until started the third execution, the Final Solution.
No, nobody worked.
Nobody worked.
It was a different ghetto than other ghettos.
We had-- we had rations.
We were on line for just little, little food.
But we were able to get-- my mother had friends, Ukrainian friends.
And somehow they were able to smuggle some food to my mother, but very little.
They did it near a gate, because we were-- the ghetto had barbed wires.
There was a gate.
They came-- they came to the gate and they were able to [INAUDIBLE].
I-- my husband will give you the information.
I just don't recall right now.
Yes, however long.
And then my husband-- at that time, he was my friend from school.
We were friends.
And he was able to, at the last execution-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] at the last execution, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we heard shots.
And then we knew that this is the Final Solution.
